nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in densely vegetated freshwater habitats across central Mexico, including high-altitude lakes, canals, ponds, streams, slow-flowing marshes, and artificial waterways. Has been reported among aquatic plant roots, leaf litter, and submerged debris, and is widely cited as tolerant of a relatively wide range of temperatures, pH, and dissolved-oxygen levels — a trait commonly linked to its long history of survival in the disturbed wetlands of the Mexico City basin.

decorPreferences: fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
abundant hiding places (clay tubes, cholla wood, coconut caves, stacked stones)
leaf litter (oak, almond, beech) for biofilm grazing and shelter
live or artificial plants tolerated — the species is generally not destructive to vegetation
tightly-fitting lid (capable of climbing filter intakes and decor)
sponge filter or shrimp-safe intake guard

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic omnivore and detritivore. Has been reported to accept commercial sinking pellets, dwarf-crayfish and shrimp foods, blanched vegetables (peas, zucchini, spinach, pumpkin), Indian almond and oak leaf litter, and protein items (bloodworms, brine shrimp, blackworms, small earthworm pieces) offered roughly two to three times per week. Calcium-rich foods or supplements (cuttlebone, blanched kale, mineral stones) are commonly recommended to support successful molting.

goodWith: small peaceful midwater fish (small tetras, rasboras, endlers, white cloud minnows)
snails (nerites, ramshorns — minor predation on juveniles possible)
robust adult dwarf shrimp at the keeper's risk
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish that may predate on the crayfish
long-finned slow fish (bettas, fancy guppies, angelfish) that can have fins nipped
other crayfish species (risk of crayfish-plague transmission and hybridisation within Cambarellus)
very small or newly-hatched dwarf shrimp (occasional predation reported)
powerful bottom-dwelling predators (large loaches, big catfish)

summary: A small, hardy dwarf crayfish endemic to the high-altitude waters of central Mexico. Generally considered peaceful, beginner-friendly, and unusually tolerant of cooler temperatures, making it a good fit for unheated or lightly-heated nano aquariums.
careNotes: Cambarellus montezumae is one of the more accommodating dwarf crayfish in the hobby. Adults reach roughly 3 cm in males and up to 3.7 cm in females, so a pair or small group (e.g. one male with two females) can be kept comfortably in a well-structured aquarium from about 40 L upward; retailer sources often cite a 20 L floor for a pair, but the larger footprint reduces molt-period aggression and gives juveniles room to grow out. The species is native to the lakes, canals, and marshes of the Valley of Mexico and surrounding states (Jalisco, Puebla, formerly Lake Texcoco and Xochimilco), where it has been documented surviving water temperatures from roughly 10 to 30 °C; in the aquarium it generally does best in the 18–26 °C range and tolerates room-temperature setups well, which is one of its main appeals over tropical Cambarellus relatives. Hard, slightly alkaline water (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 6–20, kH 3–20) is widely preferred. Provide abundant hiding places — clay tubes, cholla wood, stacked stones, leaf litter — because each crayfish needs its own retreat for molting, and post-molt individuals are extremely vulnerable to tankmates for several days. A tightly-fitting lid is generally advised; while not the dedicated escape artist that larger Procambarus crayfish are, dwarf crayfish are capable climbers. Like all crustaceans, the species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and snail treatments, which should be avoided. As an American crayfish it is a potential carrier of crayfish plague (Aphanomyces astaci) and should be kept strictly isolated from any non-American crayfish or their water. Cross-source notes: the designated primary (The Shrimp Farm) and secondary (Practical Fishkeeping) both returned HTTP 403 to WebFetch — the data above is sourced from search snippets of The Shrimp Farm's dwarf-crayfish article, the Garnelio English-language species page, the iNaturalist / Wikipedia Acocil article, and the Aquarium Breeder Cambarellus guide, all of which agreed closely on water parameters, sizing, and temperament; figures should be considered confident.
breedingNotes: Generally regarded as straightforward to breed in a well-kept species or peaceful community tank. Females have been reported to carry roughly 20–40 (occasionally up to 60) fertilised eggs glued beneath the abdomen for around 3–5 weeks, with timing strongly temperature-dependent. Young emerge as miniature crayfish that look like their parents and become independent within a few days; they remain in the substrate and leaf litter for the first few weeks. Cannibalism on smaller juveniles by larger siblings and adults has been reported, so dense plant cover, leaf litter, and plentiful hides materially improve survival; high-yield breeders may prefer to grow out batches in a dedicated rearing tank. Sexual maturity is typically reached around 2.5–3 months of age.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary source did not host a dedicated Cambarellus montezumae product/care page; husbandry data was collected from the site's '6 Dwarf Crayfish For Your Aquarium' overview article (https://www.theshrimpfarm.com/posts/6-dwarf-crayfish-for-your-aquarium/) via search snippets — direct WebFetch returned HTTP 403. Source for native range (Jalisco, Puebla, central Mexico), 10–30 °C natural tolerance, sandy-brown colouring, female 3.7 cm / male 3.1 cm size, and 20 L pair minimum.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 on the homepage and could not be navigated to a species-specific article; substituted with Garnelio, the Acocil Wikipedia entry, and Aquarium Breeder. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Garnelio — Montezuma Dwarf Crayfish
additional.notes: Substitute secondary. Source for water parameters (18–28 °C aquarium target, pH 6.8–8, gH 6–20, kH 3–20), 20 L for pair / small group tank floor, peaceful and only mildly territorial temperament, omnivorous diet, 2–3 year lifespan, 30–40 egg clutches with 3–5 week incubation, and taxonomy (Cambaridae / Decapoda).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Acocil (Cambarellus montezumae)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback and ecology cross-check: confirmed Cambaridae / Decapoda, endemic to Mexico (Jalisco and Puebla), IUCN Least Concern, tolerant of a wide range of temperature, pH, and oxygen, and traditional importance as a subsistence food (acocil) — the source of the common name.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Dwarf Mexican Crayfish guide
additional.notes: Genus-level Cambarellus reference (primarily Cambarellus patzcuarensis but applicable to montezumae): cross-checked temperature 18–24 °C optimum, pH 7.5–8.0, gH 3–18, kH 3–15, 10 gal / ~40 L pair tank floor, 2-year typical lifespan, omnivore diet, molting frequency (adults ~5×/year, juveniles every 7–10 days), and sexual maturity at 2.5–3 months.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Adults have been reported to molt several times per year (often cited around five times annually for mature Cambarellus dwarf crayfish), while juveniles molt much more frequently — roughly every 7–10 days during peak growth. Molt intervals tend to shorten at warmer temperatures and lengthen in cooler water. Post-molt individuals are soft-bodied and very vulnerable for several days; each crayfish should have its own enclosed hide. Like all crustaceans the species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and snail treatments, which should be avoided entirely; the shed exoskeleton is normally re-consumed for calcium and should be left in the tank.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Generally easy to breed in a stable, well-structured tank with hiding places and leaf litter. See top-level breedingNotes for clutch size, incubation timing, and juvenile rearing details.
